explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled, adding unnecessary network and client noise.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF allows some run-time errors to leave transactions partially applied instead of rolling back.
SELECT * returns every column from Sales.Orders, coupling callers to the table schema.
SELECT TOP (10) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary, non-deterministic set of rows from Sales.Orders.
DISTINCT on Sales.Orders.OrderID is redundant because OrderID is the primary key.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can read uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) applies a function to the indexed OrderDate column, preventing a simple range seek.
o.CustomerID (int) is compared to @CustomerIdText (nvarchar), forcing implicit conversion of the column side.
@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID prevents reliable cardinality estimation and index seeks.
LIKE pattern N'%urgent%' starts with a wildcard and cannot use an index seek on Comments.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) wraps the CustomerID column, making the predicate non-sargable.
o.OrderID + 0 performs arithmetic on the OrderID column, preventing direct index matching.
Hard-coded business identifier 1 is used in the predicate on Sales.Orders.CustomerID.
o.CustomerID <> 1 is an inequality predicate that often scans broad portions of the table.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers lacks a join predicate and multiplies the two tables.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row from the cross-joined result.
Comma-separated FROM list with the join condition in WHERE obscures the join and invites cartesian products.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row from the comma-joined tables.
Scalar COUNT(*) subquery against Sales.OrderLines is evaluated once per outer Sales.Orders row.
Correlated EXISTS against Sales.OrderLines is evaluated per outer Sales.Orders row instead of using a set-based join.
The CASE expression on o.Comments is repeated in both the SELECT list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 references a select-list position and becomes fragile when columns are changed.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by OrderDate lacks a stable tie-breaker, producing non-deterministic pages.
Hard-coded OrderID literals 1 and 2 are embedded in the UNION branches.
UNION performs duplicate removal between disjoint Sales.Orders branches that are already unique by OrderID.
Scalar COUNT(*) subquery against Sales.OrderLines is evaluated once per outer Sales.Orders row.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching Sales.OrderLines rows when only existence is needed.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID FROM Sales.Orders silently accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value.
@OrderIds is a table variable that may receive poor cardinality estimates on older compatibility levels.
SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY inserts an arbitrary set of OrderIDs into the table variable.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY copies an arbitrary set of rows into #ImplicitOrders.
Cursor order_cursor processes Sales.Orders rows one at a time instead of using a set-based operation.
WHILE loop increments a counter iteratively instead of performing set-based work.
Dynamic SQL string concatenates @CustomerID directly into the query text.
User-supplied @CustomerID is concatenated directly into executable SQL, enabling SQL injection.
EXEC (@Sql) executes dynamic SQL without bound parameters.
Dynamic SQL string concatenates @OrderID directly into the query text.
User-supplied @OrderID is concatenated directly into executable SQL, enabling SQL injection.
sp_executesql is invoked without parameter definitions, leaving the value embedded in the SQL text.
#Scratch is created explicitly and is not dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is used for new error handling instead of THROW.
PRINT is used for diagnostics and is buffered, making it unsuitable for structured logging.
Hard-coded CustomerID value 1 is embedded in the WHERE clause of Sales.Orders.
Unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in the predicate on Sales.Orders.OrderDate.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') relies on session-sensitive conversion because no style parameter is specified.
COALESCE in the join predicate on CustomerID inhibits normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row from the join.
Self-join on Sales.Orders only retrieves OrderID, which is already available in the same row.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row from the self-join.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts the result by a wide nvarchar(MAX) text column.
SUM(ol.Quantity) aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines table without a restrictive filter.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 applies a row-level predicate after grouping instead of in WHERE.
COUNT(*) aggregates the entire Sales.Orders table without a restrictive filter.
ORDER BY OrderCount is redundant because the aggregate query returns exactly one row.
MERGE on Sales.Orders has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases.
MERGE UPDATE sets target.Comments to its current value, causing logging and locking for no data change.
UPDATE sets Comments to its current value, causing logging and locking for no data change.
DELETE from Sales.OrderLines filters only by Quantity = -1, a broad non-key predicate that can remove many rows.
Hard-coded sentinel value -1 is embedded in the DELETE predicate on Sales.OrderLines.Quantity.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is not wrapped in a TRY/CATCH error-handling block.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session identity-management state.
sp_who is invoked without a schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes the session setting and is not restored.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES omits the explicit target column list.
Hard-coded CategoryID value 99 is embedded in the INSERT VALUES list.
LIKE pattern '%' + @CustomerName + '%' starts with a wildcard and prevents an index seek on CustomerName.
Dynamic SQL string concatenates @CustomerName directly into a LIKE predicate.
The constructed LIKE pattern starts with a wildcard, preventing an index seek on CustomerName.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into executable SQL, enabling SQL injection.
sp_executesql is invoked without parameter definitions.
sp_executesql is invoked without a schema qualifier.
Hard-coded CustomerCategoryID value 1 is embedded in the UPDATE SET clause.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and would modify every row.
Unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded in the predicate on Sales.Orders.OrderDate.
Predicate on the outer-joined Sales.Orders.OrderDate in WHERE converts the LEFT JOIN into an inner join.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL uses equality comparison with NULL instead of IS NULL and returns no rows.
dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) is a scalar UDF evaluated row-by-row in the SELECT list.
CATCH block selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
CATCH block is reached inside an active transaction and does not roll back before handling the error.
WITH (INDEX = 1) is an explicit, hard-coded index hint in production code.
Query uses three nested levels of derived tables and should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
LIKE pattern '%A%' starts with a wildcard and prevents an index seek on CustomerName.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF; an inline TVF would optimize better.
SELECT * returns all columns from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti, coupling callers to the function's output.
